clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 When writing scraper clients, we require a baseurl and a pattern, an example from the NOAA Client:
```python
baseurl = r'ftp://ftp.ngdc.noaa.gov/STP/swpc_products/daily_reports/solar_region_summaries/%Y/%m/%Y%m%dSRS.txt'
pattern = '{}/{year:4d}/{month:2d}/{year:4d}{month:2d}{day:2d}SRS.txt'

{"action":"opened","number":7077,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/pulls/7077","id":1406327128,"node_id":"PR_kwDOACEKh85T0tlY","html_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077","diff_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/7077","number":7077,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"merge baseurl and pattern for scraper clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

closes #7073 clients","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-06-24T19:30:07Z","updated_at":"2023-06-24T19:48:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/pulls/7077","html_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077","diff_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/7077.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"as things are right now, while defining scraper-based dataretriever clients, the `baseurl` which we send to scraper, is a pattern in the strftime format and we also require a second `pattern` string which is the same thing but formatted in the `parse` format.
What this PR does is change this to requiring only one full parse-formatted `pattern` in the scraper and generating the strftime formatted `timepattern` from it.

Having the new parse-format `pattern` around will also help considerably help simplifying many existing functions like `_extractDateURL()` which are using regex for parsing and help with #4888.

Description
The functionality within `sunpy.util.scraper` is really useful and is used within Fido for dataretriver clients. However, the way it is currently written at the moment is confusing and there seem to be several bugs and issues following some discussion at the community meeting (see also #4493). It is also particularly confusing to use with the `regex` keyword and hence makes it challenging to write new clients. 

This issue is to open a discussion about the redesigning of the scraper for a potential GSOC project.

see also #4336

Hello @dstansby - as mentioned here are the details for accessing Proba-2 in-situ DSLP data:

An example query:
- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/tap/sync?REQUEST=doQuery&LANG=ADQL&FORMAT=JSON&PHASE=RUN&QUERY=SELECT * FROM p2sa.v_file WHERE  (file_date >= '2014-10-01 00:00:00')   AND   (file_date <= '2014-10-31 00:00:00')   AND   (file_type  in  ('DSLP_DERIVED'))  ORDER BY file_date ASC

There is also the file_type 'DSLP_CALIBRATED' which I presume are some calibration files. Not sure if they are also of interest.

In both cases, this will give you a vo-table json file with the metadata required to then download the files:

```
{
  "metadata": [
    {
      "name": "calibrated",
      "datatype": "BOOLEAN"
    },
    {
      "name": "extension",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_date",
      "datatype": "TIMESTAMP",
      "xtype": "adql:TIMESTAMP"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_path",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_size",
      "datatype": "BIGINT"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_type",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "processing_level",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    }
  ],
  "data": [
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPA_20141001.cdf",
      "136478",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19815408",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf",
      "136479",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19788276",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
...
```
I think you can ignore most of it and if you prefer only select on the fields you want in the original query which will reduce the size of the json if you see what I mean.

You can then use the file_oid or the file_name to download the data. The file_name is probably more obvious to the naked eye e.g:

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_oid++in+%28136483%29

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_name='DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf'

The DSLP data is between 2009 and 2016.

Any doubts, please let me know. You have my contact details. I wouldn't mind having a go at this one but probably won't have time in the next couple of weeks.

Thanks
Jonathan

investment"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-01-06T15:57:53Z","updated_at":"2023-02-05T07:48:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Originally opened by @joncook at https://github.com/heliopython/heliopy/issues/945 - I'm transferring this to sunpy.

Hello @dstansby - as mentioned here are the details for accessing Proba-2 in-situ DSLP data:

An example query:
- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/tap/sync?REQUEST=doQuery&LANG=ADQL&FORMAT=JSON&PHASE=RUN&QUERY=SELECT * FROM p2sa.v_file WHERE  (file_date >= '2014-10-01 00:00:00')   AND   (file_date <= '2014-10-31 00:00:00')   AND   (file_type  in  ('DSLP_DERIVED'))  ORDER BY file_date ASC

There is also the file_type 'DSLP_CALIBRATED' which I presume are some calibration files. Not sure if they are also of interest.

In both cases, this will give you a vo-table json file with the metadata required to then download the files:

```
{
  "metadata": [
    {
      "name": "calibrated",
      "datatype": "BOOLEAN"
    },
    {
      "name": "extension",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_date",
      "datatype": "TIMESTAMP",
      "xtype": "adql:TIMESTAMP"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_path",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_size",
      "datatype": "BIGINT"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_type",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "processing_level",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    }
  ],
  "data": [
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPA_20141001.cdf",
      "136478",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19815408",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf",
      "136479",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19788276",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
...
```
I think you can ignore most of it and if you prefer only select on the fields you want in the original query which will reduce the size of the json if you see what I mean.

You can then use the file_oid or the file_name to download the data. The file_name is probably more obvious to the naked eye e.g:

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_oid++in+%28136483%29

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_name='DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf'

The DSLP data is between 2009 and 2016.

Any doubts, please let me know. You have my contact details. I wouldn't mind having a go at this one but probably won't have time in the next couple of weeks.

Thanks
Jonathan

+"""
+===============================
+Querying and loading SHARP data
+===============================
+
+In this example we will demonstrate how to acquire `Spaceweather HMI Active Region Patch (SHARP) <http://jsoc.stanford.edu/doc/data/hmi/sharp/sharp.htm>`__ data and load it into a `sunpy.map.Map`.
+"""
+import os
+
+import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
+
+import astropy.units as u
+
+import sunpy.map
+from sunpy.net import Fido
+from sunpy.net import attrs as a
+
+###################################################################
+# To search for SHARP data, we will need to query the `JSOC <http://jsoc.stanford.edu/>`__.
+# We will use `Fido <sunpy.net.fido_factory.UnifiedDownloaderFactory>`
+# and make use of the JSOC attributes that allow us to query the JSOC.
+#
+# Exporting data from the JSOC requires registering your email first.
+# Please replace this with your email address once you have registered.
+# See `this page <http://jsoc.stanford.edu/ajax/register_email.html>`__ for more details.
+jsoc_email = os.environ["JSOC_EMAIL"]
+
+result = Fido.search(a.Time("2011-03-09 23:20:00", "2011-03-09 23:30:00"),
+                     a.Sample(1*u.hour),
+                     a.jsoc.Series("hmi.sharp_cea_720s"),
+                     a.jsoc.PrimeKey("HARPNUM", 401),
+                     a.jsoc.Notify(jsoc_email),
+                     a.jsoc.Segment("Bp"))
+print(result)
+
+###################################################################
+# Next, we can download the file
+
+file = Fido.fetch(result)
+
+###################################################################
+# Now that we have the file, we can construct a `sunpy.map.Map` and plot You can see [this section](https://docs.sunpy.org/en/latest/dev_guide/contents/newcomers.html#setting-up-a-development-environment) of the Newcomer's Guide, in particular [this](https://docs.astropy.org/en/latest/development/workflow/development_workflow.html#add-changelog) part of the astropy workflow docs.
- The `codecov` test is about writing tests for any new code that you've added. Since the PR is only about an example entry and no changes in sunpy functionality have been made i suppose you can ignore this.
- You can fix the `pre-commit` test by either installing and setting up the pre-commit hook with git or by commenting `pre-commit.ci autofix` and have the bot fix it for you

Describe the feature

I'm currently running the code that scrapes CDAWeb for currently supported datasets. The code can be found here: https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/blob/8c0b9054dc25918ee5b6286a93edbbca09cde903/sunpy/net/cdaweb/helpers.py#L118

The code only fires off one request at a time, waiting for it to come back before putting in the next request. This means it takes a long time (>~5 mins) to scrape all the datasets. I think it *should* be possible to send multiple requests at once to speed this up.

investment"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2022-01-06T15:57:53Z","updated_at":"2023-02-19T07:46:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Originally opened by @joncook at https://github.com/heliopython/heliopy/issues/945 - I'm transferring this to sunpy.

Hello @dstansby - as mentioned here are the details for accessing Proba-2 in-situ DSLP data:

An example query:
- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/tap/sync?REQUEST=doQuery&LANG=ADQL&FORMAT=JSON&PHASE=RUN&QUERY=SELECT * FROM p2sa.v_file WHERE  (file_date >= '2014-10-01 00:00:00')   AND   (file_date <= '2014-10-31 00:00:00')   AND   (file_type  in  ('DSLP_DERIVED'))  ORDER BY file_date ASC

There is also the file_type 'DSLP_CALIBRATED' which I presume are some calibration files. Not sure if they are also of interest.

In both cases, this will give you a vo-table json file with the metadata required to then download the files:

```
{
  "metadata": [
    {
      "name": "calibrated",
      "datatype": "BOOLEAN"
    },
    {
      "name": "extension",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_date",
      "datatype": "TIMESTAMP",
      "xtype": "adql:TIMESTAMP"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_path",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_size",
      "datatype": "BIGINT"
    },
    {
      "name": "file_type",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_name",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    },
    {
      "name": "instrument_oid",
      "datatype": "INTEGER"
    },
    {
      "name": "processing_level",
      "datatype": "VARCHAR"
    }
  ],
  "data": [
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPA_20141001.cdf",
      "136478",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19815408",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
    [
      "false",
      "CDF",
      "2014-10-01 00:00:00.0",
      "DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf",
      "136479",
      "\\/p2sa\\/p2saps\\/science\\/dslp\\/dslp_derived\\/2014\\/10",
      "19788276",
      "DSLP_DERIVED",
      "DSLP",
      "3",
      "N\\/A"
    ],
...
```
I think you can ignore most of it and if you prefer only select on the fields you want in the original query which will reduce the size of the json if you see what I mean.

You can then use the file_oid or the file_name to download the data. The file_name is probably more obvious to the naked eye e.g:

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_oid++in+%28136483%29

- http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/data?retrieval_type=PRODUCT&QUERY=SELECT+*+FROM+p2sa.file+where+file_name='DERIVED_SLPB_20141001.cdf'

The DSLP data is between 2009 and 2016.

Any doubts, please let me know. You have my contact details. I wouldn't mind having a go at this one but probably won't have time in the next couple of weeks.

Thanks
Jonathan

It helped tremendously! Since the PROBA2 servers are out of maintenance now, I've made some progress and would like some input...

*forgive the length of the reply it's pretty straightforward :sob:*

the DSLP data is from 2009-11-18 to 2016-03-31. At once, it returns only 1000 queries. Also complex SQL queries involving `GROUP_BY` or aggregate functions of any kind aren't supported so I had to rely on manually going through the data in roughly sets of two years by changing the years in the file_date queries which is how I learned the previous information and that the `processing_level` column is indeed **always N/A.**

A quick script to verify:
```python
for year in range(2009, 2016, 2):
    response = requests.get(f"http://p2sa.esac.esa.int/p2sa-sl-tap/tap/sync?REQUEST=doQuery&LANG=ADQL&FORMAT=JSON&PHASE=RUN&QUERY=SELECT * FROM p2sa.v_file WHERE (file_date >= '{year}-01-01 00:00:00') AND (file_date <= '{year+2}-01-01 00:00:00') AND (file_type in ('DSLP_DERIVED')) ORDER BY file_date ASC")
    # print(response.json())
    data = response.json()['data']
    print(len([row for row in data if row[10] != 'N\\/A']))
```

There are following fields which are supported (in this order, would help with indexes in records if anyone wants to verify):
`calibrated`, `extension`, `file_date`, `file_name`, `file_oid`, `file_path`, `file_size`, `file_type`, `instrument_name`, `instrument_oid`, `processing_level`.

out of which the following have constant values:
`calibrated`: 'false'
`extension`: 'CDF'
`file_type`: 'DSLP_DERIVED'
`instrument_name`: 'DSLP'
`instrument_oid`: 3
`processing_level`: 'N\\/A'


I have the following questions:
- This leaves us with `file_date`, `file_name`, `file_oid`, `file_path` and `file_size` for unique values out of which I think we don't need to make a new attr out of? (except `file_date` which is already done with `a.Time`) Should there be support for any one of these as an attr to search using?
- another point of discussion is which of these should show up in the `QueryResponseTable` response? (since they'll all be redundant and repeating the same value) Currently every field is included.
- also the answer to these questions could depend upon whether we want to use this source for the other instruments' data it provides, namely SWAP and LYRA.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/comments/1448218347/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Documentation Link

https://matplotlib.org/devdocs/

### Problem

Our install instructions say to install from conda, but conda's version of matplotlib isn't always the latest. For example:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300499/201185589-18cc82d6-0656-4b63-954b-be3563c1a044.png)

But this won't work because the latest version of matplotlib on conda is [3.5.3](https://anaconda.org/anaconda/matplotlib), which is not obvious unless you know to look at the page. 

### Suggested improvement

Maybe we should change it to `conda install -c conda-forge matplotlib` since we can ensure that will work? Currently the file detection for gzipped-FITS files relies on checking if they are named in the `filename.fits.gz` format. However since this condition isn't a necessity for gzipped files, this approach misses those gzipped-FITS files which do not end with the `.gz` extension.

### Proposed solution

{"action":"opened","number":6693,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/pulls/6693","id":1187140460,"node_id":"PR_kwDOACEKh85GwlNs","html_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/6693","diff_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/6693.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/pull/6693.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/6693","number":6693,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"detect gzipped-FITS files by magic-bytes","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

I think it'd make more sense to have only one test for gzip-fits files, the latter one since if it passes then there's no need to test for the first one. 

Should I remove the first test (renaming the second one to `test_read_file_fits_gzip()`)? If yes, it'd also make the three test `-fits.gz` files redundant, should i delete those too?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/comments/1374157914/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

PR Description
Detection for gzipped-FITS files is now done by checking file signatures instead of checking for file extensions.
First a gzip file-signature is checked and if found, the file's first few bytes are decompressed and checked for the FITS file signature.
The PR closes  #6692.

We know asking good questions takes effort, and we appreciate your time.
Thank you.

Please be aware that everyone has to follow our code of conduct:
https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/blob/master/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.rst

Also that these comments are hidden when you submit this github issue.

Please have a search on our GitHub repository to see if a similar issue has already been posted.
If a similar issue is closed, have a quick look to see if you are satisfied by the resolution.
If not please go ahead and open an issue!
-->

### Description
<!--
Provide a general description of the feature you would like.
If you prefer, you can also suggest a draft design or API.
This way we have a deeper discussion on the feature.
-->
SWPC provides lots of different data files in JSON including both of the solar cycle and predicted solar cycle files that are provided by the NOAA fido clients. They can be found [here](https://services.swpc.noaa.gov/json/). A new client called SWPC should be created which replaces and expands on the NOAA client.

### Additional context
<!--
Add any other context or screenshots
This part is optional.
Delete this section heading if you do not use it.
-->
This may really push on the capabilities of Fido and how it is structured if we wanted to include most of the data sources provided here.

maps, from any level (as far as I can tell), do not include a `BUNIT` keyword or any equivalent keyword to encode the unit information.\r\n\r\nHowever, according to Table 1.1 in [the XRT analysis guide](https://xrt.cfa.harvard.edu/resources/documents/XAG/XAG.pdf), the units for the different levels are DN for level 0 and DN/s for level 1. However, there is also a note that level 1 can be DN or DN/s, depending on which options are passed to `xrt_prep`. When level 1 data is divided by the exposure time (to be put in units of DN/s), there is a tag added to the `HISTORY` that indicates that the data have been renormalized. When pulling in the data, the source could check for this tag and set the unit appropriately and otherwise fall back to DN.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/6716/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/6716/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/comments/1387474791","html_url":"https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/issues/6716#issuecomment-1387474791","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/6716","id":1387474791,"node_id":"IC_kwDOACEKh85Ssy9n","user":{"login":"exitflynn","id":37214399,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM3MjE0Mzk5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/37214399?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn","html_url":"https://github.com/exitflynn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exitflynn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-18T17:49:04Z","updated_at":"2023-01-18T17:49:04Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I'd like to give this a shot! {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/pulls/comments/1083005615","pull_request_review_id":1264276913,"id":1083005615,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOACEKh85AjVqv","diff_hunk":"@@ -289,6 +289,23 @@ An Example of ``register_values()``
 
         return adict
 
+Registering custom attrs in the ``attrs`` namespace
+---------------------------------------------------
+
+When you have custom attrs defined in a separate attrs module, you can add them to the namespace using the :meth:`~sunpy.net.BaseClient._attrs_module` class method.
+The method returns a tuple of length 2, where the first element is the name you want to add to the main attrs namespace. The second is module where the custom attrs exist, so that can be imported.
+An example for this can be seen as implemented in the JSOC client:
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    @classmethod
+    def _attrs_module(cls):
+        return 'jsoc', 'sunpy.net.jsoc.attrs'
+
+This adds the attrs that exist within ``sunpy.net.jsoc.attrs`` such as ``Keyword`` which can now be accessed by the man attrs import, e. g., ``a.jsoc.Keyword``.
+
+This import can be any module, external as {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/pulls/comments/1083010597","pull_request_review_id":1264285054,"id":1083010597,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOACEKh85AjW4l","diff_hunk":"@@ -289,6 +289,23 @@ An Example of ``register_values()``
 
         return adict
 
+Registering custom attrs in the ``attrs`` namespace
+---------------------------------------------------
+
+When you have custom attrs defined in a separate attrs module, you can add them to the namespace using the :meth:`~sunpy.net.BaseClient._attrs_module` class method.
+The method returns a tuple of length 2, where the first element is the target module name under which you want to add the custom attrs to the main attrs namespace. +Registering custom attrs in the ``attrs`` namespace
+---------------------------------------------------
+
+When you have custom attrs defined in a separate attrs module, you can add them to the namespace using the :meth:`~sunpy.net.BaseClient._attrs_module` class method.
+The method returns a tuple of length 2, where the first element is the target module name under which you want to add the custom attrs to the main attrs namespace. Describe the feature

I'm currently running the code that scrapes CDAWeb for currently supported datasets. The code can be found here: https://github.com/sunpy/sunpy/blob/8c0b9054dc25918ee5b6286a93edbbca09cde903/sunpy/net/cdaweb/helpers.py#L118

The code only fires off one request at a time, waiting for it to come back before putting in the next request. This means it takes a long time (>~5 mins) to scrape all the datasets. I think it *should* be possible to send multiple requests at once to speed this up.

### Proposed solution

tackle!"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-01-18T17:42:01Z","updated_at":"2023-01-24T14:38:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"XRT maps, from any level (as far as I can tell), do not include a `BUNIT` keyword or any equivalent keyword to encode the unit information.

However, according to Table 1.1 in [the XRT analysis guide](https://xrt.cfa.harvard.edu/resources/documents/XAG/XAG.pdf), the units for the different levels are DN for level 0 and DN/s for level 1. However, there is also a note that level 1 can be DN or DN/s, depending on which options are passed to `xrt_prep`. When level 1 data is divided by the exposure time (to be put in units of DN/s), there is a tag added to the `HISTORY` that indicates that the data have been renormalized. +                    unit = u.astrophys.dn / I figured I'd do a quick progress report on 
### what I've managed to implement so far:
1. The requests/downloads during the updation happen concurrently. It takes about 10 minutes to update the data for me now
2. I've also put a [Semaphore](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-sync.html#asyncio.Semaphore) in [there](https://github.com/exitflynn/sunpy/blob/paralyzed/sunpy/net/cdaweb/helpers.py#L144) which can be used to limit the number of concurrent processes at a moment.

### Problems I've been facing:
- I can't figure out why it seems to go out with a connection lost error by the time it reaches the end of the list. :flushed: 
- Also, despite limiting to 5 connections, some servers error out, and right now the way I'm getting around that is by using a dirty hack which basically involves waiting for 5 secs and trying the same thing again, hoping some request gets completed in the meanwhile and decreases the load there. :clown_face: 
- Aand currently what i've done is limit <=5 connections _from_ a client whereas it should be limited to <=5 connections _to_ a particular server so as to not put too much load there (no problem with >5 connections from the user's client). I'll have to look into how one would implement that. :thinking: 


To test these changes, you'll only need to run `tools/update_attrs_json.py`. :grimacing: :innocent: 

[There are some helpful print statements in there for debugging purposes which I'll remove later on. I also removed updating the VSO and JSOC datasets for the duration of the PR since we're concerned with changes in CDAweb database alone for now (Though this could be implemented in the other databases too if this is something that we end up going out)]
In case anyone has any suggestions about any improvements I can make to my approach here that'd be great!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sunpy/sunpy/issues/comments/1410542720/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

SHARP data series is frequently used, but we do not have an example showing how to query the data or how to construct a Map for the data. ~~Currently, our `JSOCClient` does not support this, but~~ it can be done through `drms` directly, e.g. adapting the [example here](https://docs.sunpy.org/projects/drms/en/stable/generated/gallery/plot_synoptic_mr.html) ```python import astropy.time import drms c = drms.Client() sharp_num = 377 ts = astropy.time.Time('2011-02-10 22:00:00',scale='tai') ds = f'hmi.sharp_cea_720s[{sharp_num}][{ts.tai.isot}_TAI/10h@1h]' results, filenames = c.query(ds, key=drms.const.all, seg=['Bp','Br','Bt']) ``` Then, to download the first file for the phi component of the field ('Bp'), ```python import astropy.io.fits data = astropy.io.fits.getdata(f'http://jsoc.stanford.edu{filenames["Bp"][0]}') header = dict(results.iloc[0]) m = sunpy.map.Map(data, header) m.peek() ``` It may be worth looking into adding support through our JSOC client for this kind of query as well as adding support for these types of maps, either through a standalone map class or through the existing HMIMap
